retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins; therefore, it is no surprise that collagen is comprised of amino [4] acids. The primary amino acid sequence of collagen is glycine-proline-X or glycine-X-hydroxyproline [5] X can be any of the other 17 amino acids, and every third amino acid is glycine.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Clinical Significance -- Scurvy
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis is made clinically, DNA studies and collagen mutation studies can be used as adjuncts. The prognosis largely depends on the type of EDS a patient has. However, there is no cure for EDS, and treatment is supportive.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Clinical Significance -- Scurvy
retrieved_docs.content: This is a family of genetic disorders that affect the bones making them weak and easily breakable. Inheritance is autosomal dominant, and most cases are due to mutations in the COL1A1 or COL1A2 genes. There are 8 types, each with differing degrees of severity with type 1 the mildest and type II the most severe. [7] Gene mutations affect procollagen formation in which the small glycine amino acid is substituted for bulkier amino acids which alter the collagen triple helix structure.

retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Synthesis and Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The synthesis of collagen begins with DNA translation of a polypeptide chain in the rough endoplasmic reticulum of intracellular space (See Figure. Review of Collagen Synthesis). This polypeptide chain is called preprocollagen, composed of alpha chains with repeating amino acid glycine-X-Y sequences (X and Y are proline or lysine). [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Clinical Significance -- Scurvy
retrieved_docs.content: A nutritional deficiency of water-soluble vitamin C or ascorbic acid most commonly causes scurvy. Scurvy is rare in the developing world and is mostly seen in infants, the elderly, and alcoholics, all who may have inadequate nutritional intake and malnutrition. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Clinical Significance -- Scurvy
retrieved_docs.content: OI affects 1 in 15,000 people, and diagnosis is made clinically and can be confirmed by DNA or collagen testing. Prognosis depends on the type of OI. There is no cure and treatment is supportive and based on the prevention of fractures. Bisphosphonates, surgery, and physiotherapy have been shown to help. Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (EDS )

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Synthesis and Structure
retrieved_docs.content: The proline and lysine residues of the alpha chain become hydroxylated with the help of vitamin C-dependent hydroxylases; hydroxylation helps form a stable structure. Vitamin C deficiency results in impaired hydroxylation of pre-procollagen and defective collagen synthesis, a condition known as scurvy. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Clinical Significance -- Scurvy
retrieved_docs.content: Patients may present with general fatigue, weakness, poor wound healing, anemia, and gum disease. Clinically, one of the first signs of scurvy occurs on the skin and manifests as perifollicular hemorrhage where follicles of the skin are plugged with keratin. These areas appear as bruise-like spots around the hair follicles. There can also be fragile hairs arranged in a corkscrew confirmation. Scurvy is diagnosed clinically with a dietary history, and x-rays may show subperiosteal hemorrhage or cortical thinning. Ascorbic acid levels less than 11 micromols/L can help confirm the diagnosis. Treatment is vitamin C supplementation and a diet that includes tomatoes, citrus fruits, and other vegetables high in vitamin C. Osteogenesis Imperfecta (OI )

retrieved_docs.title: Osteogenesis Imperfecta -- Pathophysiology -- Defects Involving Type 1 Collagen Molecules
retrieved_docs.content: Frameshift mutations (involving premature stop codon in the affected allele) can result in a quantitative decrease in the amount of structurally normal type 1 collagen. Patients who are heterozygous for this condition may secrete half the normal amount of type 1 collagen [haplo-insufficiency, as seen in type IA OI in Sillence Classification]. [9] Alternatively, errors in substitution or deletion involving a glycine peptide residue along the polypeptide chain can result in the production of structurally or qualitatively abnormal or less effectual collagen. The phenotypic expression of these defects depends on the position of substitution, whether glycine substitutes at the carboxy-terminal (severe form) or the amino-terminal (milder form) of the polypeptide chains. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Fibril Assembly
retrieved_docs.content: Lysyl oxidase a copper-dependent enzyme acts on lysine and hydroxylysines, and covalent bonding between tropocollagen molecules form a collagen fibril

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Clinical Significance -- Scurvy
retrieved_docs.content: Depending on the genetic mutation and type of collagen affected patients may show an array of clinical findings; however, there are some common manifestations in EDS patients that range from mild to life-threatening.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: As discussed earlier, there are issues of concern in the biochemical synthesis of collagen. Errors of collagen synthesis can present with clinical manifestations. A few notable diseases are scurvy, osteogenesis imperfecta, and Ehlers–Danlos syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Synthesis and Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Next, tropocollagen is reinforced with many adjacent tropocollagen molecules by covalent cross-linking of hydroxylated lysine residues, resulting in collagen fibrils. The cross-linking of hydroxylysine residues is made possible through a copper-dependent lysyl oxidase enzyme. Finally, several collagen fibrils accumulate to form a thick bundle; the end product is collagen fibers. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Connective Tissue -- Cellular Level -- Collagen Synthesis and Structure
retrieved_docs.content: Next, the hydroxylated lysine of the alpha chain undergoes glycosylation with the addition of a carbohydrate. After glycosylation, the formation of disulfide bonds and hydrogen bonds between 3 different alpha chains forms a triple helix. The resultant structure is now procollagen. Defective triple helix formation leads to osteogenesis imperfecta and impaired bone matrix synthesis. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Collagen Synthesis -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Collagen is composed of 3 chains. The chains are wound together to form a triple helix. Since glycine is the smallest of all the amino acids, it allows the chain to form a tight configuration, and and it can withstand stress.

retrieved_docs.content: an ingredient alongside arginine, histidine, isoleucine, leucine, methionine, phenylalanine, threonine, tryptophan, and valine. Across all these products, lysine is consistently described using the relationships “has_active_ingredient,” “has_active_moiety,” or “has_ingredient,” indicating that it is an essential and deliberately included amino acid in parenteral nutrition formulations. Its presence alongside other essential amino acids and, in some cases, glucose, electrolytes, and stabilizing agents underscores lysine’s critical role in supporting protein synthesis, tissue repair, and overall metabolic needs of patients who require intravenous nutritional support.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2001
retrieved_docs.content: Amino Acids, I.V. solution additive are linked to Antifibrinolytic amino acids, indicating that certain intravenous amino‑acid formulations possess antifibrinolytic activity. Antifibrinolytic amino acids are also referred to by the term antifibrinolytika, reflecting their classification as agents that inhibit fibrinolysis. This relationship highlights the therapeutic relevance of using amino‑acid IV additives to provide antifibrinolytic effects in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 132
retrieved_docs.content: Collectively, the knowledge base underscores lysine’s dual role as a pharmacologically active amino acid in parenteral nutrition and antifibrinolytic preparations, and as a non‑active excipient in diverse dermatologic and cosmetic products, while also highlighting the broader utilization of comprehensive amino‑acid mixtures—including tryptophan—in both systemic and topical therapeutic contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 427
retrieved_docs.content: Amino Acids, I.V. solution additive is a formulation that incorporates alanylglutamine, a dipeptide derived from the dipeptidy structure. This intravenous additive is also linked to the class of Antifibrinolytic amino acids, a broader group that includes specific agents referred to as antifibrinolytika. Together, these components highlight the role of alanylglutamine as a peptide‑based ingredient within IV amino‑acid solutions and the relevance of antifibrinolytic amino acids, including antifibrinolytika, for their potential to inhibit fibrinolysis in clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2000
retrieved_docs.content: Alanylglutamine is a dipeptide that is mapped from its constituent amino acids, indicating that it originates from the combination of alanine and glutamine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2052
retrieved_docs.content: ENZYMES FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS are classified as a type of PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS. The broader category PREPARATIONS FOR TREATMENT OF WOUNDS AND ULCERS includes CICATRICANTS, which are agents that promote scar formation and facilitate wound healing. Consequently, enzymes used in wound and ulcer therapy belong to the larger group of cicatricant preparations, highlighting their role within the spectrum of therapeutic agents aimed at promoting tissue repair.
